---
title: "Installation Guide"
author: "Anne Stahlfeld & Tobias Holden"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
---

## Installation Methods

Below are instructions on how to run emodpy from the terminal on our AWS
SLURM cluster, or other linux-based clusters. The instructions are specific to
our AWS setup but can easily be adapted to other clusters or HPCs that use SLURM
scheduling. A shared, up-to-date virtual environment already exists on the
cluster (see **Option A**), so most participants do **not** need to install
anything. If you would prefer to manage your own environment or a specific
version of emodpy/idmtools, follow **Option B**.

### Prerequisites, Login, Modules

1. Review prerequisites and recommendations.
    a. https://docs.idmod.org/projects/idmtools/en/latest/platforms/slurm/index.html#prerequisites
    b. https://docs.idmod.org/projects/idmtools/en/latest/platforms/slurm/index.html#recommendations

2. Login/SSH to the cluster. Connect using the method provided for the program
   (SSH, or the AWS session manager / web terminal you were given). A direct SSH
   from a terminal prompt looks like the following:

    ``` bash
    ssh -i <path-to-your-key> <username>@13.58.119.211
    ```

    - *NOTE: connect with the SSH key file and username issued to you by the
      program organizers (replace `<path-to-your-key>` and `<username>`; add
      `-X` if you want X11 forwarding). Your home directory on the cluster is
      `~` (i.e. `/shared/home/<username>`), and this is where you should clone
      the `FE-2026-examples` repository.*

3. Singularity/Apptainer (used by emodpy-malaria tasks to run EMOD) is provided
   as an environment module. The run scripts load it for you automatically via
   `manifest.singularity_module`, so you do not normally need to load it by
   hand. To use it interactively:

    ``` bash
    module use /shared/emod/shared_tools/modulefiles
    module load singularity
    ```

4. The Singularity image used to run EMOD is bundled with this repository at
   `containers/dtk_run_al2023.sif` and is referenced by `manifest.SIF_PATH`, so
   there is nothing to download.

    - *NOTE: the older `dtk_run_rocky_py39.sif` (the image used on the previous
      cluster) is built on a Rocky base whose glibc is too old for the
      pip-installed Eradication binary, which targets Amazon Linux 2023. The
      custom `dtk_run_al2023.sif` image (built from `containers/dtk_run_al2023.def`)
      matches that base and is what you should use on AWS.*

### Option A: Use the shared environment (recommended)

A shared virtual environment (Python 3.11) already has emodpy-malaria,
idmtools, and the SLURM platform installed. Just activate it — no installation
required:

``` bash
source /shared/emod/emodpy-malaria/.venv/bin/activate
```

It is convenient to add an alias to your `.bashrc` so you can activate it with a
single command:

``` bash
alias load_emodpy='source /shared/emod/emodpy-malaria/.venv/bin/activate'
```

After adding the alias, run `source ~/.bashrc` once, then simply type
`load_emodpy` whenever you want to activate the environment.

### Option B: Build your own virtual environment – `SLURM_LOCAL` mode

If you would rather manage your own environment, create one in your home
directory. The system `python3` (>= 3.7, required by idmtools) is sufficient;
no Python module needs to be loaded on AWS.

1. Create and activate a Python virtual environment (named "myvenv" here — you
   may use any name):

    ``` bash
    python3 -m venv myvenv
    source ./myvenv/bin/activate
    ```

    - *NOTE: The following step is only needed when a new idmtools Singularity
      image is available and you've previously installed packages in your home
      directory. It clears local packages from previous installations inside the
      Singularity container:*

    ``` bash
    rm -fr ~/.local/lib/python*
    ```

2. Install emodpy-malaria and the idmtools SLURM platform:

    ``` bash
    pip3 install emodpy-malaria --index-url=https://packages.idmod.org/api/pypi/pypi-production/simple

    pip3 install idmtools_platform_slurm --index-url=https://packages.idmod.org/api/pypi/pypi-production/simple
    ```

    - *NOTE: some weeks also use `idmtools-calibra` and `seaborn` (Week 3
      calibration). Install them with the same index URL if you build your own
      environment; the shared environment already includes them:*

    ``` bash
    pip3 install idmtools-calibra seaborn --index-url=https://packages.idmod.org/api/pypi/pypi-production/simple
    ```

    - *NOTE: The above installs emodpy-malaria and its dependencies (idmtools,
      emod-api, etc.). For more on `pip install` and virtual environments, see
      https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/guides/installing-using-pip-and-virtual-environments/#using-requirements-files*

## Setting up SSH access to GitHub

To `git clone` this repository and to `git push` your own work back to GitHub
**from the cluster**, the cluster needs to be able to authenticate to your
GitHub account. The recommended way is an SSH key: it is created once, its
**private** half stays on the cluster, and its **public** half (safe to share)
is added to your GitHub account.

1. Check whether you already have a key. If this prints a key, skip to step 3:

    ``` bash
    cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
    ```

2. If you do not have one, generate it (press Enter at every prompt to accept
   the defaults; a passphrase is optional):

    ``` bash
    ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your_email@example.com"
    ```

3. Print your **public** key and copy the entire line (starts with
   `ssh-ed25519` and ends with your email):

    ``` bash
    cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
    ```

    - *NOTE: only ever share the `.pub` (public) file. Never share
      `~/.ssh/id_ed25519` (the private key), which must stay on the cluster.*

4. Add it to GitHub: go to **GitHub → Settings → SSH and GPG keys →
   New SSH key**, give it a title (e.g. "AWS cluster"), paste the line you
   copied, and click **Add SSH key**.

5. Test the connection. A successful test greets you by your GitHub username:

    ``` bash
    ssh -T git@github.com
    ```

6. Make sure your repository uses the SSH address (not HTTPS) so pushes use the
   key. Inside your clone:

    ``` bash
    git remote set-url origin git@github.com:<your-github-username>/FE-2026-examples.git
    ```

    - *NOTE: when you first clone, use the SSH address from the green **Code**
      button on GitHub (it starts with `git@github.com:`), e.g.
      `git clone git@github.com:<your-github-username>/FE-2026-examples.git`.*

7. You can now push your work:

    ``` bash
    git push
    ```
